evidence_quotes.quote: End User License Agreement that even physical media "must be destroyed" by the user if they end the service.
unfair EULAs that try to strip away their rights.
Terms of Service have been used to completely redefine the meanings of "own", "ownership" etc.

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act (DFA) presents a unique opportunity for the European Union to strengthen and update the Right to Property enshrined in Article 17 of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights, making sure European consumers are protected from abusive behaviours taking pace in the ever prevalente platforms that sell digital goods. The ongoing and upcoming European Citizen's Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames" (with already over 1 million signatures) begins a conversation that this DFA has the opportunity to deepen. More and more products - be it videogames, music, films or series - are being sold only in digital format. The convenience of it has led people to opt for that way of purchasing those things rather than through physical media. Unfortunately, publishers, developers and companies have taken advantage of that to strip away the property rights that consumers had over the copy of the goods they purchased. They have switched to "licensing" schemes by which they claim the user only gets a "license" to play the game, license which can be unilaterally taken away at the company's whim. Even when buying physical media, users are coerced to agree to unfair EULAs that try to strip away their rights. Company's "Terms of Service" have been used to completely redefine the meanings of "own", "ownership" etc. Places like California have had to issue legislation already to force companies to ditch what is, by all intents and purposes, deceitful marketing (Assembly Bill 2426 passed in 2024). Companies like the French videogame publisher Ubisoft have gone as far as to put in their End User License Agreement that even physical media "must be destroyed" by the user if they end the service. This practice has basically eroded the concept of property rights and has deceived MILLIONS of customers in Europe who are NOT aware that they're simply renting digital products. Companies keep "selling" their products as if they were legal copies that the user can buy and own forever (as was the practice for decades and decades). To make clear the ridiculousness of what's happening in the entertainment industry, imagine you buy a car and, a year later, because the manufacturer decides the model wasn't profitable enough, they not only discontinue production of said model, they go to your home and take away the car you bought or, like Ubisoft suggests, force you to destroy your own car. Ridiculous, right? But that's exactly what's happening with films, series, videogames, music etc. Often companies - specially videogame companies - have used this digitalisation to try to get people to buy multiple copies of the game product only to maintain access to it. The DFA has, here, the unique opportunity to make clear that when a European citizen BUYS a digital product, they're entitled to a copy of said product to own in perpetuity. Should the removal of the digital product be deemed essencial by the publisher, then Article 17 must be respected and the buyer must be reimbursed by the publisher/seller. European citizens are evermore attuned to this issue and expect solutions that protect their rights and rightful expectations, as well as protection from the ever growing power of media corporations and tech giants. European Institutions have, here, an unique opportunity to not only show they are listening to its Citizens but also, once again, take the lead worldwide in ensuring consumer rights and protections are in place to avoid the abuse of power most entertainment giants enjoy nowadays.
